linearizability.py - reference impl. All numbers below feed LINEARIZABILITY.md.
Pure Python stdlib. Single register X, initial value 0.

========================================================================
  SECTION A: a linearizable history - read sees the completed write
========================================================================
Register X starts at 0. Two clients, two operations:

  C1: .w==]....   W1(write,1,ret=ok)[1-4]
  C2: .....r=].   R1(read,,ret=1)[5-7]

  C1 writes X=1 (W1), completing at t=4. C2 reads X (R1),
  invoking at t=5 - AFTER W1's response. Real time says W1 happened
  first, so R1 MUST see X=1.

Real-time partial order (resp(A) <= inv(B) => A before B):
  W1 -> R1   (resp(W1)=4 <= inv(R1)=5)
  (no other edges: only 1 op-pair is time-ordered.)

Checker: 1 topological sort(s) of the real-time order; 1 legal register sequence(s):

  W1 R1   :  [0] W1(->1) R1(=1 ok)   [LEGAL  -> linearizable]

Verdict: HIST_A is LINEARIZABLE.
The read returned 1, which is exactly the value the completed write
stored. The linearization (W1, R1) is the one true sequential story.

[check] HIST_A linearizable?  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION B: a NON-linearizable history - stale read (ret=0)
========================================================================
Same setup as Section A, but now R1 returns 0 (stale) even though it
started AFTER W1 completed:

  C1: .w==]....   W1(write,1,ret=ok)[1-4]
  C2: .....r=].   R1(read,,ret=0)[5-7]

  C1 writes X=1, completing at t=4. C2 reads at t=5 and gets 0.
  Real time forces W1 before R1, so R1 should see 1. It saw 0.

Checker: 1 topological sort(s); 0 legal:

  W1 R1   :  [0] W1(->1) R1(=0 MISMATCH ret=0)   [ILLEGAL -> stale]

Verdict: HIST_B is NOT linearizable.
The ONLY real-time-consistent order is (W1, R1); replaying it makes
R1 expect 1, but it returned 0. No linearization exists.

--- contrast with SEQUENTIAL CONSISTENCY (Lamport 1979) ---
Sequential consistency drops the real-time clause, keeping only
program order (each client's own ops stay ordered). Now R1 is free
to be placed BEFORE W1, since they are on different clients:

  W1 R1   :  [0] W1(->1) R1(=0 MISMATCH ret=0)   [illegal]
  R1 W1   :  [0] R1(=0 ok) W1(->1)   [LEGAL -> seq. consistent]

Sequential verdict: HIST_B is sequentially consistent.
The order (R1, W1) is legal: R1 reads the initial 0, THEN W1 writes 1.
The client SAW W1 finish, but sequential consistency lets the system
pretend R1 'hadn't noticed' it yet. LINEARIZABILITY forbids exactly this.

[check] HIST_B not linearizable?  OK
[check] HIST_B IS sequentially consistent?  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION C: the linearization point - one instant per operation
========================================================================
Each operation has a [inv, resp] interval. The linearization point is
the SINGLE instant inside that interval where the operation 'appears
to execute' atomically. Line up the points in time and you get the
linearization. Real time only constrains NON-overlapping ops; ops that
overlap may have their points in either relative order.

History C (two CONCURRENT writes + a read):

  C1: .w====]....   W1(write,1,ret=ok)[1-6]
  C2: ..w==].....   W2(write,2,ret=ok)[2-5]
  C3: .......r=].   R1(read,,ret=2)[7-9]

  W1 [1--6] and W2 [2--5] OVERLAP: neither finishes before the other
  starts, so there is NO real-time edge between them. R1 [7--9] starts
  after both finish, so both must precede it.

Real-time sorts: 2 (W1/W2 may swap; R1 always last). Legal: 1.

  W1 W2 R1   :  [0] W1(->1) W2(->2) R1(=2 ok)   [LEGAL]
  W2 W1 R1   :  [0] W2(->2) W1(->1) R1(=2 MISMATCH ret=2)   [illegal (read would see wrong value)]

Verdict: HIST_C is LINEARIZABLE.

One valid linearization:  W1 W2 R1
Matching each op to a linearization point inside [inv, resp]:

| op  | kind  | inv | resp |  interval  | lin pt |
|-----|-------|-----|------|------------|--------|
| W1  | write(1) | 1   | 6    | [1,6]     | 3      |
| W2  | write(2) | 2   | 5    | [2,5]     | 4      |
| R1  | read(ret=2) | 7   | 9    | [7,9]     | 8      |

The points line up in time as: W1@3 < W2@4 < R1@8
Each point lies inside its op's interval, points respect real-time
order, and replaying them gives a legal register story. THAT is the
linearization-point definition of linearizability (Herlihy & Wing 1990).

[check] HIST_C linearizable with valid lin points?  OK
[check] linearization points are strictly increasing in time:  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION D: the checker - enumerate real-time orderings, test each
========================================================================
Wing & Gong (1993): a history is linearizable iff SOME topological
sort of the real-time partial order is a legal sequential register
history. The algorithm is a backtracking enumeration:

  1. Build the real-time partial order (resp(A) <= inv(B) => A->B).
  2. Enumerate every total order (topological sort) of that order.
  3. Replay each as a register; if ANY is legal, -> linearizable.

Run it on all four histories:

| history | #ops | #real-time sorts | #legal | linearizable? |
|---------|------|------------------|--------|---------------|
| HIST_A  | 2    | 1                | 1      | YES           |
| HIST_B  | 2    | 1                | 0      | NO            |
| HIST_C  | 3    | 2                | 1      | YES           |
| HIST_D  | 2    | 2                | 1      | YES           |

Detail for HIST_D (overlapping write+read, read returns initial 0):
  C1: .w===].   W1(write,1,ret=ok)[1-5]
  C2: ..r=]..   R1(read,,ret=0)[2-4]

  W1 [1--5] and R1 [2--4] OVERLAP -> no real-time edge -> 2 sorts:

    W1 R1   :  [0] W1(->1) R1(=0 MISMATCH ret=0)   [illegal]
    R1 W1   :  [0] R1(=0 ok) W1(->1)   [LEGAL -> linearizable]

  (R1, W1) wins: R1 reads the initial 0, THEN W1 writes 1. The read
  happened to complete before the write 'took effect'. This is the key
  freedom linearizability gives for CONCURRENT operations.

GOLD verdicts (pinned for linearizability.html):
  is_linearizable(HIST_A) = True  (1 legal / 1 sorts)
  is_linearizable(HIST_B) = False  (0 legal / 1 sorts)
  is_linearizable(HIST_C) = True  (1 legal / 2 sorts)
  is_linearizable(HIST_D) = True  (1 legal / 2 sorts)

[check] all four verdicts match expected {'HIST_A': True, 'HIST_B': False, 'HIST_C': True, 'HIST_D': True}:  OK
GOLD scalar: is_linearizable(HIST_B) = False  (must be False)
GOLD scalar: #legal sorts HIST_D = 1  (must be 1)

========================================================================
  SECTION E: implementation cost - linearizability needs coordination
========================================================================
Why isn't everything linearizable? Because guaranteeing that every
read sees the latest write forces replicas to COORDINATE before they
respond. The store must pick a single 'latest' value, and that needs
agreement - CONSENSUS (Raft, Paxos, ZAB) or an equivalent quorum.

CAP consequence (Gilbert & Lynch 2002): linearizability + availability
are INCOMPATIBLE under a network partition. A linearizable store must
REFUSE requests it cannot keep consistent -> it is 'CP', not 'AP'.

| system            | mechanism            | linearizable? | cost / notes                |
|-------------------|----------------------|---------------|-----------------------------|
| etcd              | Raft consensus (quorum) | YES           | leader + majority round-trip per write |
| ZooKeeper         | ZAB (Paxos variant)  | YES (sync read) | default reads are sequential; sync() for linearizable |
| Spanner           | Paxos + TrueTime     | YES (externally consistent) | GPS/atomic clocks; 2PC across shards |
| Cassandra (ONE)   | tunable / sloppy quorum | NO            | eventual; a read may hit a stale replica |
| Dynamo            | vector clocks, eventual | NO            | AP store; conflicts resolved after the fact |

Read the rows as a COST LADDER:
  - etcd / ZooKeeper / Spanner: pay Raft/Paxos round-trips + need a
    live QUORUM; during a partition the minority side STOPS (CP).
  - Cassandra / Dynamo: skip coordination, serve any replica fast;
    reads can be stale, but the store stays AVAILABLE under partition (AP).

Linearizability is the strongest SINGLE-OBJECT model (Herlihy & Wing
1990). Stronger models (sequential consistency is weaker; serializability
is about TRANSACTIONS across multiple objects) are out of scope here.

RULE OF THUMB:
  linearizable  =>  consensus / quorum  =>  extra latency + CP on partition
  eventual      =>  no coordination      =>  fast + AP on partition

[check] every 'YES' row lists a consensus/quorum mechanism:  OK

========================================================================
  GOLD CHECK: linearizable passes, non-linearizable fails
========================================================================
  HIST_A (read sees completed write)               -> LINEARIZABLE     (1 legal / 1 sorts)  [OK]
  HIST_B (stale read after completed write)        -> NOT linearizable (0 legal / 1 sorts)  [OK]
  HIST_C (concurrent writes, read sees later)      -> LINEARIZABLE     (1 legal / 2 sorts)  [OK]
  HIST_D (overlapping R/W, read sees initial)      -> LINEARIZABLE     (1 legal / 2 sorts)  [OK]

[check] GOLD: all 4 verdicts correct:  OK

[check] HIST_B: linearizable=False, sequentially_consistent=True  (seq is weaker)  ->  OK
  This is the exact gap: sequential consistency ignores real time,
  linearizability enforces it. That clause is the whole definition.

========================================================================
  DONE - all sections printed
========================================================================
